scenario_name: Scenario 21 — Multi-Parcel Tracking Partial Delivery Detection
justification: All pass criteria met: (1) Shipment vs Parcel clearly distinguished as aggregate root vs child entity with separate lifecycles. (2) Shipment status explicitly and repeatedly described as derived/projected from parcel states — BR-3 states 'no public setter or direct mutation path', BR-4 defines precise derivation rules. (3) 8 parcel-level states defined (CREATED, PICKED_UP, IN_TRANSIT, OUT_FOR_DELIVERY, DELIVERED, FAILED_ATTEMPT, LOST, RETURNED) with full state machine transitions in BR-5. (4) Business invariants cover partial delivery detection (BR-4), lost parcel escalation (BR-7), and parcel-level dispute (BR-6). (5) 8 non-trivial edge cases surfaced including concurrent scans, lost-then-found, single-parcel shipments, and max retry thresholds. (6) 12 detailed acceptance criteria in Given/When/Then format.

observations.text: Derivation rule is the centerpiece of the analysis — BR-3 and BR-4 make the critical domain insight explicit and testable, with precise rules for every shipment state combination
State machine is comprehensive with 8 parcel states and explicit terminal state enforcement, including the non-obvious FAILED_ATTEMPT→OUT_FOR_DELIVERY retry loop
Distinguishes invariants from business policies (e.g., BR-6 dispute targeting is marked non-invariant with justification), showing genuine domain reasoning rather than blanket rules
Acceptance criteria are thorough — AC-9 covers concurrent scan race conditions, AC-10 handles single-parcel edge case, AC-3 covers mixed terminal states
Business impact analysis identifies concrete migration risks (breaking API contracts, in-flight shipment migration) and stakeholder impact across 6 groups
Confidence section honestly flags low-confidence areas like dispute resolution workflow and carrier-specific transition mapping
Missing explicit time-window escalation rule — how long after partial delivery before the system escalates (e.g., remaining parcels stuck in transit for N days)
Lost-then-found scenario edge case rejects the transition but doesn't define a recovery workflow — only says 'manual investigation', which is thin for a predictable logistics scenario
Refund/reshipment logic is mentioned in edge cases but lacks formal business rules — no BR for refund granularity at parcel level, only dispute filing (BR-6)

agent_output_excerpt: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"Refactoring from shipment-level tracking to parcel-level tracking to enable partial delivery detection. The current system treats a Shipment as a monolithic unit with one tracking number and one status, which cannot represent real-world scenarios where individual parcels within a shipment have independent lifecycles (different carriers, different delivery times, losses, disputes). This is a fundamental domain model correction — the current model is not fai
